question: Describe the primary function of the average pooling layers in the LeNet-5 architecture and explain why they were used instead of the max pooling layers more common in modern CNNs.
expectedPoints: To reduce the spatial dimensions (height and width) of the feature maps, decreasing computational complexity and providing a slight translational invariance.
They compute the average value within each pooling receptive field.
Yann LeCun's original design used average pooling as it was better suited for the task and the datasets at the time, acting as a form of noise suppression.

question: Identify the key innovation of LeNet-5 that distinguished it from previous neural network designs and enabled its success on image recognition tasks.
expectedPoints: Its use of a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture.
The application of shared weights and local receptive fields through convolution operations to significantly reduce the number of parameters and efficiently extract spatial hierarchies of features.

question: Walk through the complete forward pass of the LeNet-5 architecture, detailing the sequence and purpose of each type of layer (C, S, F) from input to output.
expectedPoints: Sequence: C1 (convolution) -> S2 (avg pooling/subsampling) -> C3 (convolution) -> S4 (avg pooling/subsampling) -> C5 (convolution, interpreted as a fully-connected layer) -> F6 (fully-connected) -> Output (fully-connected, using RBF units in original).
Purpose of C layers: Feature extraction via learned kernels.
Purpose of S layers: Dimensionality reduction and translation invariance.
Purpose of F layers: High-level reasoning and classification.
